coredata.dc:title: Simple and selective determination of 6-thioguanine by using polyethylenimine (PEI) functionalized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Hongyan
Li, Li
Ding, Yaping
Zhuang, Qi
coredata.dc:description:
In this work, a new selective and simple fluorescence probe based on polyethylenimine(PEI) functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDs) for fast determination of 6-thioguanine (6-TG) was developed. We successfully prepared the highly fluorescent PEI-CDs by hydrothermal method, and completed the synthesis and modification processes in one step. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized CDs was 38% and higher than that of most previous reports (5–30%). The fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs decreased obviously with gradually increased concentration of 6-TG. The interference substances caused a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of the (PEI-CDs)-6-TG reaction system with the interference ratios all below 2.8%. Under optimum conditions, a great linear relationship between fluorescence intensity function log(F
0
/F) and concentration of 6-TG in a wide range from 4μM to 800μM with a detection limit (S/N = 3) of 1.33μM was obtained. And this proposed approach was successfully applied for the determination of 6-TG in human serum and urine samples. Furthermore, the PEI-CDs fluorescence probe has superior potential in practical application of detecting 6-TG due to its inexpensive precursors, simple operation, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
6-thioguanine
Fluorescence probe
Selective determination
High fluorescence quantum yield

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of 6-TG in phosphate buffer solution
Influence of pH and reaction time

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21671132 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the PEI-CDs. (B) The particle size distribution histogram of PEI-CDs. (C) The HRTEM image of as-prepared CDs.
(A) a) UV–Vis absorption, b) fluorescence excitation and c) fluorescence emission spectra of PEI-CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of PEI-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (360–500nm).
FT-IR spectra of a) pure PEI and b) PEI-CDs.

(A) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs in the (1) absence and (2) presence of 6-TG. (Inset shows the relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) at different pHs.) (B) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of (PEI-CDs)-6-TG system.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PEI-CDs with addition of different concentrations of 6-TG from 0 to 800μM (0, 4, 12, 30, 50, 70, 100, 130, 160, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800μM). (B) The relationship between log(F0/F) and 6-TG concentration in the range of 4–800μM, where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of 6-TG, respectively. (C) The relationship between log(F0/F) and 6-TG concentration in the range of 4–200μM.

(A) Ratio of the fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of the PEI-CDs before (F0) and after (F) the addition of various interferences. (B) Relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of the PEI-CDs in the presence of 200μM 6-TG with the coexistence of other interferences. (Concentrations of Na+, Zn2+ are 1200μM, concentrations of all other interferences are 600μM.).
Illustration of the synthesis of PEI-CDs and the determination of 6-TG based on the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent PEI-CDs.
Comparison of the proposed method with other reported methods for the detection of 6-TG.
Quantitative determination of 6-TG in real samples.

head.ce:title: Simple and selective determination of 6-thioguanine by using polyethylenimine (PEI) functionalized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shanghai University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this work, a new selective and simple fluorescence probe based on polyethylenimine(PEI) functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDs) for fast determination of 6-thioguanine (6-TG) was developed. We successfully prepared the highly fluorescent PEI-CDs by hydrothermal method, and completed the synthesis and modification processes in one step. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized CDs was 38% and higher than that of most previous reports (5–30%). The fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs decreased obviously with gradually increased concentration of 6-TG. The interference substances caused a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of the (PEI-CDs)-6-TG reaction system with the interference ratios all below 2.8%. Under optimum conditions, a great linear relationship between fluorescence intensity function log(F
0
/F) and concentration of 6-TG in a wide range from 4μM to 800μM with a detection limit (S/N = 3) of 1.33μM was obtained. And this proposed approach was successfully applied for the determination of 6-TG in human serum and urine samples. Furthermore, the PEI-CDs fluorescence probe has superior potential in practical application of detecting 6-TG due to its inexpensive precursors, simple operation, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility.

Illustration of the synthesis of PEI-CDs and the determination of 6-TG based on the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent PEI-CDs.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: 6-Thioguanine (6-TG), one of the common thiopurine anti-cancer drugs, anti-tumor drugs and immunosuppressive agents, is widely used as therapeutic agents in the clinical treatment of acquired immune deficiency syndrome, acute childhood lymphoblastic leukemia, inflammatory bowel disease, and other conditions [1,2,4]. 6-TG is a sulfur derivative of guanine [3] and metabolically converted to 2′-deoxy-6-thioguanosine 5′-triphosphate, a substrate of DNA polymerase, that is incorporated into DNA [4]. The 6-TG incorporated into DNA can undergo oxidation and methylation of the exocyclic sulfur atom, that leads to mismatches during the replication, repair system activation, cell-cycle arrest, and cell death [2,4]. Due to the great treatment effects of 6-TG as anticancer therapeutic agents, many methods for the detection of 6-TG have been developed such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5–7], surface plasmon resonance(SPR) [8], and electrochemical methods [9,10]. However, most of these methods have some disadvantages including the complex operations, expensive reagents and installation, cumbersome materials preparation, and time consumption. Therefore, developing a simple, fast, and sensitive approach to the detection of 6-TG is still necessary.

As we all know, the fluorescence method has many advantages compared to others mentioned above, such as simple operation, fast outcome, high sensitivity and low cost for the trace analysis. Quantum dots play an important role in most fluorescence sensors. Generally, the fluorescent quantum dots can be divided into two categories, namely, semiconductor quantum dots (such as CdTe [11], CdSe [12], ZnSe [13], PbS, etc) and carbon quantum dots [14,15]. As compared to semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (Carbon dots, CDs) show more advantages including stable fluorescence, easy modification process, low toxicity, and so on.

Carbon dots (CDs), with a quasi-spherical structure smaller than 10nm, are stable light-emitting carbon nanomaterial [16]. The molecular weight of CDs is generally thousands or tens of thousands. The cores of carbon dots are generally composed of sp2 carbon nanocrystalline or amorphous carbon [17]. Moreover, there are lots of water-soluble functional groups on the surfaces of CDs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl, thus CDs have good solubility in water and easy to be modification. Since Xu et al. [18] firstly discovered carbon dots in 2004, they were flourished in various fields such as biomedicine [19,20], bioimaging [21], energy conversion and storage [22,23], and catalysis [24], due to their great water solubility, photochemical stability, anti-photobleaching ability, good biocompatibility and strong fluorescence. Furthermore, CDs have also been widely used as fluorescent nanosensors. For example, Liu et al. [25] used carbon dots as sensitive fluorescence probes for the determination of kaempferol. Hou et al. [26] prepared a simple fluorescent sensor based on L-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots for the detection of methyl parathion. Barati et al. [27] used carbon dots as fluorescence probes for selective determination of hemoglobin. However, to the best of our knowledge, the application of polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDs) as fluorescent nanosensors for simple and selective detection of 6-TG has not been reported.

Polyethylenimine (PEI) is a hydrophilic polymer with numerous active amine groups both in the backbone and branched chains [28]. The abundant of amino groups can generate strong hydrogen bonding with organic compounds containing oxygen- or nitrogen-functional group. In these cases, more attention has been paid to the long-chain PEI in biosensor [29], drug delivery [30], pesticide residue analysis [31], water treatment [32] and so on. Moreover, the water solubility, stability, fluorescence property and biocompatibility of carbon dots have been well improved with the modification of PEI [33,34], it can be expected to have the bright prospect of the biomedicine application with the potential of PEI-CDs in biological cells [34,35].

In this work, we proposed a novel fluorescence probe based on PEI-CDs for the fast and selective determination of 6-TG (as shown in Scheme 1). The obtained fluorescence probes were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, making the synthesis and modification processes realized in one step. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized CDs was 38% and higher than that of most previous reports (5–30%) [36–38]. Meanwhile, the PEI-CDs were used as the fluorescent probes for the determination of 6-TG in phosphate buffer solution (PBS), and even in human serum and urine samples, exhibiting not only good sensitivity but also satisfactory practicability.

Polyethylenimine (PEI) (ethylenediamine branched, average Mw = 800), ethylene glycol (98%), and phosphoric acid were all purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. 6-Thioguanine (6-TG, 98%+) was purchased from Adamas Rragent Co. Ltd. Dipotassium hydrogen phosphate trihydrate (AR), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (AR), sodium hydroxide (AR) were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All chemicals were directly used without further purification.

An RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) were used to obtain the fluorescence emission spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the PEI-CDs were recorded by using a JEOL-200CX transmission electron microscope (Japan). And high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the as-prepared PEI-CDs were obtained by using a JEOL JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (Japan). Via an UV-2501PC spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), the ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was recorded. And the fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were acquired from an AVATAR 370 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (America).

The carbon dots were synthesized according to a literature method with some modifications [39]. In brief, ethylene glycol (10ml), polyethylenimine (PEI, 0.5ml), ultra-pure water (2ml), and phosphoric acid (3.5ml) were added to the beaker in sequence. Then the mixture was stirred for 2h, and transferred into a dry Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (30ml) and heated at 180°C for 24h. After heating, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The obtained carbon dots solution was filtered and centrifuged to remove large particles. The upper solution was collected and purified by using the dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff of 1000, USA) against deionized water for 18h three times to remove the unreacted substances. The dialysate was stored as a stock solution at 4°C.

The fluorescence quantum yield of PEI-CDs was measured and calculated according to the following formula:
QY
u
=
QY
s
(
F
u
A
s
n
u
2
)
/
(
F
s
A
u
n
s
2
)
where the subscripts u and s represent the samples and standards which need to be tested, respectively. QY is fluorescence quantum yield, F is integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance of the solution at the excitation wavelength, and n is the refractive index of the solution. In this experiment, the sample is PEI-CDs aqueous solution, and the standard was quinoline sulfate dissolved in 0.1M sulfuric acid (H2SO4) solution which QY is reported to be 54% [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 6-TG in phosphate buffer solution
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation procedures for the 6-TG stock solution (5mM) were as follows: 20.9mg 6-TG powders were transferred to a 25ml volumetric flask and added some deionized water. Since 6-TG is easily dissolved in the alkaline solution, 2ml of 0.1M NaOH solution is added and then diluted the solutions to the scale line with deionized water and mixed sufficiently. The stock solution was stored at 4°C until later use.

The procedures for detection of 6-TG were as follows: different concentrations of 6-TG solution were added in a series of 25ml volumetric flasks, and then incubated in 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 6.5) for several minutes. After the above operation, we continuously added 200μL of as-prepared PEI-CDs stock solution and diluted the solutions to the scale line with PBS (pH = 6.5) and mixed sufficiently. At last, we monitored the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system after 15min at room temperature. In this experiment, the excitation wavelength of the fluorescence emission spectra is 360nm and the slit width is 5nm.

The TEM image of as-prepared PEI-CDs is shown in Fig. 1A, which demonstrates that PEI-CDs were homogeneous quasi-spherical nanoparticles. However, the lattice fringes of PEI-CDs couldn’t be observed in HRTEM image (Fig. 1C), indicating the as-modified CDs may be amorphous. As can be seen in Fig. 1B, the corresponding size distribution histogram clearly shows that most as-synthesized carbon dots are distributed in the range of 6–10nm.

In order to explore the optical properties of the PEI-CDs, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra are acquired. As shown in Fig. 2A, the PEI-CDs typically display optical absorption in the UV–Vis region with an absorption peak at 353nm, and a strong emission peak at 440nm appeared upon excitation at 360nm. What is worth noting for the photoluminescence (PL) of CDs is that the emission wavelength and intensity is dependent on the excitation wavelength. Fig. 2B depicts the emission spectra are red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelength, which has the same phenomenon in comparison with the formerly reported CDs [41,42]. All of these indicated that the PEI-CDs have excellent optical performance.

FT-IR spectroscopy was recorded to characterize relevant functional groups of the as-prepared PEI-CDs in order to determine the successful modification of PEI. As shown in Fig. 3, the broad absorption band at 3401cm−1 indicates the existence of -OH and N-H, and the strong peak located at 1633cm−1 is attributed to N-H bending vibration of -NH2. The PEI-CDs show some characteristic peaks of PEI, such as N-H (3401cm−1 and 1633cm−1), C-H (1460cm−1) and C-N (1124cm−1) [43], which indicates that the PEI was successfully capped on the surface of CDs. The hydroxyl group of alcohol can be readily substituted by amine at high temperature (i.e. > 150 centigrade), thus the PEI should attach to the glycol through the “N” atom. The reaction between the ethylene glycol and PEI should be amination. Furthermore, The passivation of PEI improves the water solubility, biocompatibility and luminescence intensity of the carbon dots [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH and reaction time
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of exploring the optimal detecting environment of fluorescent probes and to promote the maximization of sensing effect, the optimization experiments of pH and reaction time was carried out in this work. As depicted in Fig. 4A, in the absence and presence of 6-TG, the fluorescence intensity was monitored as a function of pH to investigate the influence of pH (4.0–10.0) on the sensor system.

Fig. 4A(1) displays that the fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CDs varies with the change in pH without 6-TG. In general, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots decreases with the increase of pH, however, a significant enhancement was found at pH 6.5, which presents several times by repeated experiments, indicating that the emission intensity of CDs is pH dependent.

Fig. 4A(2) illustrates that the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs together with 120μM 6-TG also changes with the change of pH. The intensity decreases slightly in pH range of 5.0–7.5, and then increases obviously with pH increases from 7.5 to 10.0. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system tends to be steady at pH 6.5–7.5, and the difference between two fluorescence intensity (F
0 and F) was maximum at 6.5 within the tested pH range, where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of 6-TG, respectively. To obtain the optimal pH value more precisely, the relative fluorescence intensity (F
0
/F) at different pHs is displayed in Fig. 4A(inset). At pH 4.0 and 6.5, their F
0
/F are comparatively large, while the acidity of pH 4.0 is strong, with consideration of practical applications, we chose 6.5 as the optimum pH for our experiment. The fluorescence intensity of the system is greatly increased and gradually approaches the intensity of PEI-CDs after pH 7.5, this may be due to the change of the charge of the carbon dots [32], which affects the reaction between PEI-CDs and 6-TG.

As shown in Fig. 4B, after adding 6-TG, the fluorescence intensity of the system decreased sharply within a minute, and then remained stable since 15min. The further increase in reaction time has little effect on the intensity, so the reaction time should be at least 15min before detecting.

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots decreased obviously with the gradual increase of 6-TG concentration, while the peak pattern of the fluorescence spectrum doesn’t change, as demonstrated in Fig. 5A. In addition, the linear relationship of the quenching effect between PEI-CDs and 6-TG is shown in Fig. 5B, from which it could be seen that the quenching of fluorescence intensity of the system is proportional to the concentration of 6-TG in the range from 4μM to 800μM, and the corresponding linear equation is log(F
0
/F) = 0.0013[C6-TG] + 0.0463, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs at 360nm in the absence and presence of 6-TG, respectively. The correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.9946, and the limit of detection was determined to be 1.33μM (S/N = 3). As shown in Fig. 5C, another linear relationship was obtained at a narrower concentration range of 6-TG from 4μM to 200μM with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9985, the corresponding linear equation is log(F
0
/F) = 0.0016[C6-TG] + 0.011. If you would like to detect 6-TG within the low concentration, it is better to choose the second equation to obtain more accurate results.

In addition, we compared the analytical performance of our developed approach with some other reported methods for the detection of 6-TG in Table 1. The linear range of the proposed method is wider than other methods except the amperometric sensor. And the developed method is simple, cost-effective, rapid and selective that benefits for its practical use. The potential interferences at a concentration up to 600μM caused negligible (< 2.8%) effects on the (PEI-CDs)-6-TG reaction system ( Fig. 6B), indicating the excellent selectivity of this fluorescent probe. Furthermore, the stock solution of PEI-CDs was stored in 4°C for about 4 months, the fluorescent intensity still maintained almost unchanged (< 5%). Based on the above unique advantages of this as-proposed method, which further suggest the significant potential for the detection of 6-TG in body fluids, such as human serum and urine.

6-TG greatly quenches the fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CDs with the increase in the concentration of 6-TG and shown in Fig. 5A. Since the surface of PEI-CDs contains a lot of -NH- and -NH2 groups, it is possible that 6-TG binds strongly with these functionalities of PEI-CDs. Carbon dots are known to be excellent electron acceptors and donors [44], thus, the fluorescence quenching maybe attributed to an electron transfer process from the photo-excited PEI-CDs to the aromatic heterocycle groups in 6-TG. In other words, The mechanism for 6-TG detecting may be attributed to a wrapping of the PEI-CDs by the 6-TG, allowing an effective non-radiative energy transfer process [25].

This possible mechanism also can be supported by the phenomenon showed in Fig. 4A (2): when the pH of the reaction environment is higher than 7.5, the fluorescence intensity of (PEI-CDs)-6-TG system is greatly increased and gradually approaches the intensity of PEI-CDs. The isoelectric points of PEI-CDs were found near pH 7.5 from the relevant report [32], and we obtained the same conclusion by using the Zetasizer 3000HS, the pKa of 6-TG is 7.44 [45], so the as-synthesis CDs and 6-TG are both negative charged (at pH > 7.5). As is known to all, like charges repel each other, the PEI-CDs are more difficult to be wrapped by 6-TG when pH > 7.5.

Superior selectivity and sensitivity are necessary and important for fluorescent probes. In order to demonstrate the selectivity of PEI-CDs on determination of 6-TG in phosphate buffer solution, we carried out the following two experiments: (A) In the absence of the test substance (6-TG), the impacts of the interference substances on the as-prepared carbon dots were examined; (B) the effects of potential interfering substances were investigated under the condition of adding a quantitative amount of 6-TG. As shown in Fig. 6, the interferences included metal ions (K+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+), some anions (CO3 2-, NO3 -, SO4 2-, Cl-), relevant amino acids (cysteine, valine, tyrosine, glycine, alanine), and other related substances (urea, ascorbic acid, glucose, sucrose, galactose, glutathione), which probably exist in the real samples were examined. It is clearly demonstrated in Fig. 6 that there is little interference from commonly existed substances (the interference ratios are all below 5.5% and 2.8% for the PEI-CDs and the reaction system, respectively), indicating the excellent selectivity of PEI-CDs as fluorescent probes for detecting 6-TG. Interference ratio = [(F
0
/F)’-(F
0
/F)] / (F
0
/F), where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of 6-TG, respectively, (F
0
/F)’ is the fluorescence intensity fraction with the adding of interference substances.

In order to verify the applicability of this fluorescent probe in real samples, different concentrations of 6-TG in human serum and urine samples were examined. The serum samples are provided by the school infirmary, which have been centrifuged at low temperature, and all the samples were obtained from different healthy volunteers. The urine samples were obtained from healthy laboratory colleagues. For fluorescence measurements, 1ml of the serum / urine solution was diluted to about 7ml with PBS (pH = 6.5) in 10ml volumetric flasks, 200μL of PEI-CDs solution and a certain amount of 6-TG were added into the above-mentioned serum / urine solution, then diluted the solutions to the scale line with PBS (pH = 6.5) and mixed sufficiently. Standard addition method was used according to the usual operating procedure with three duplicates. As shown in Table 2, the relative standard deviations (RSD) are all lower than 5%, and the recoveries vary from 94.8% to 101.5%, which illustrates the feasibility of PEI-CDs as fluorescent probes for 6-TG detection in actual samples.

In summary, we successfully developed a simple and cost-effective strategy for the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of high luminescent PEI-CDs. The as-synthesized PEI-CDs with good water solubility and strong PL were employed as fluorescence probes for the determination of 6-TG with a wide linear response range of 4–800μM. Compared with the previously reported methods for 6-TG detection, the proposed approach has some analytical merits such as convenient operation, rapid detection, good sensitivity, superior selectivity, low cost and good biocompatibility. Furthermore, satisfactory results were obtained when detecting 6-TG in human serum and urine samples, indicating the promising application of PEI-CDs as selective biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21671132).
